narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10, LSAT score of 26, part-time enrollment status, and school deciles from Year 1 (2nd decile) and Year 3 (1st decile)—and calculated a 78% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers, signaling weaker performance relative to others at a similar stage. Your LSAT score was the second strongest driver toward a failure prediction, as it fell well below the typical score of around 37 seen in the dataset. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided the third biggest influence but in the opposite direction, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing since, despite being lower than the average of 3.21, it offered some counterbalance. Compared to typical students, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles and score much stronger on the LSAT, your academic indicators stood out as riskier, even though part-time status and family income played lesser roles. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment highlighting where your profile diverges from those who historically pass the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the norm, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and exploring programs that support part-time students with academic coaching. Building a stronger narrative around your undergraduate performance with explanations or recent improvements could also help in reapplying. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas—your path ahead is still very much open with focused effort.
